GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "Super easy beauty tricks from a Vogue-approved make-up artist", "Including a whole look, done just with lipstick.", "To celebrate the new-look beauty counters at David Jones Elizabeth Street, Vogue let David Jones ambassador Victoria Lee and make-up artist Filomena Natoli loose among the luxuriously renovated floor. The pair, both passionate lovers of beauty, played with their favourites to come up with two perfect make-up looks you'll want to copy. Natoli chose Gucci Beauty Rouge a Levres Matte in Queen Christina to paint onto lips, using a brush as "it allows you to control the amount of lipstick you apply. I like to then buff it in and press the lips together to make it look more lived-in," she explains. For a softly exaggerated lip line, Natoli suggests pressing lips together over the natural lip line for a hint of a plumpness. She used the same brilliantly bright shade to give an apricot blush to Victoria's cheeks. "Use your thumb as a stamp, because it's the perfect size for your cheek. And keep it high. Anything lower than where your cheekbones contour in, is too low for blush," Natoli explains. The peachy orange of Queen Christina, with its intense pigment and matte finish, would be perfectly pretty on the eyes, says Natoli. But for a more casual take on day make-up, she opted to switch to Gucci Beauty Rouge a Levres Satin Lipstick in Susan Nude. "It's just a staple nude, that you can pat onto the lids as a tint. It gives a wash of hydration to the eye. And it's so easy, you could do this look in the car," she laughs. To dial any make-up look up in volume, Natoli says you need to first concentrate on the base. She used Guerlain L'Essentiel Primer Base first. "It's a serum-based primer which fills in pores and really just sets the skin up for foundation," she explains. Another professional kit favourite is the iconic Giorgio Armani Luminous Silk Foundation. "It's a staple for me, especially for evening looks. It has longevity, but it's also really nice and dewy. It still looks like skin," she says. For the extra drama that evening looks (and low lighting) call for, Natoli opts for "nontouring", rather than contouring. "You never want it to be obvious," she says of the technique, which is about giving the face subtle dimension. Her favourite way? A fine bronzing powder applied right under the cheekbones. As a final touch, Natoli gave Lee's blue eyes a brown smoke. "It has a French cool girl vibe that I love," she explains. Natoli used a combination of MAC's Art Library Palettes to first lay a base, define the socket line and then add smokiness both above and below the eyes."
